board.title: Operating Systems
board.author: Julius Boateng
board.description: Operating systems manage limited resources and hide significant complexity by providing useful abstractions. Techniques like multitasking and virtual memory allow many programs to share hardware resources efficiently.

clues.question: Allocates limited CPU time among competing threads; made time-sharing systems practical in the 1970s and 1980s
Signal that forces the CPU to pause the current thread to handle an event; enables async hardware communication
Divides a process' address space into variable-sized segments; separates code, data, and stack regions
Rewrote Unix in C in 1973; enabled Unix to run on different hardware architectures
Request from a process to the operating system for privileged operations like file I/O
Translates operating system requests into hardware device-specific operations
Running instance of a program with its own memory and resources
Created the original Unix operating system at Bell Labs in 1969
Lock that ensures only one thread can access shared data at a time
Divides a process' address space into fixed-sized pages; foundational to modern virtual memory implementation
Limits the number of threads allowed to access a shared resource; introduced by Dijkstra in 1962
Abstraction that organizes persistent storage as files and directories; hierarchical directories were introduced in Multics in the 1960s
State where threads wait indefinitely for resources held by other threads
Smallest unit of execution within a process
